name: First Steps: What to Do After Inhaling Toxic Chemicals at Work
description: First Steps: What to Do After Inhaling Toxic Chemicals at Work - Utah County

step.name: Step 1
step.text:
First Steps: What to Do After Inhaling Toxic Chemicals at Work

Inhaling toxic fumes or chemicals at work is a medical emergency that often turns into a legal battle. At Just Work Comp Law, we see many workers who wait too long to act, only to have their claims denied later. Here is exactly what you need to do to protect your health and your legal rights.

1. Get to Fresh Air and Seek Medical Help Immediately

Your health is the priority. If you feel dizzy, short of breath, or have a burning sensation in your chest, leave the area immediately. Go to an Urgent Care or Emergency Room. Tell the medical staff exactly what you inhaled. This creates a contemporaneous medical record—the most important piece of evidence in your case.

2. Formally Report the Incident to Your Supervisor

In Utah and Idaho, 'notice' is a legal requirement. Do not just mention it in passing to a coworker. Send an email or text to your manager so there is a paper trail. Use clear language: 'I am reporting a workplace injury. I inhaled [Chemical Name] today and am going to the doctor.'

3. Secure the Safety Data Sheet (SDS)

Every employer is required by OSHA to keep Safety Data Sheets for all chemicals on-site. Ask for the SDS for the substance you breathed in. This document lists the health hazards, symptoms, and required first aid. Show this to your doctor so they can perform the correct diagnostic tests.

4. Identify Witnesses and Documentation

Did a pipe burst? Was the ventilation turned off? Write down the names of anyone who saw the incident or who was also exposed. Take photos of the work area if possible. If your employer didn't provide a respirator or if the gear was faulty, document that as well.

5. Do Not Give a Recorded Statement to Insurance Yet

The insurance adjuster will call you soon. They may seem friendly, but they are looking for reasons to deny your claim. They might ask, 'Have you ever had allergies?' or 'Do you smoke?' These are trap questions. You have the right to speak with a lawyer before giving any recorded statements.

6. File Your Formal Claim

Reporting to your boss is not the same as filing a claim with the state. Ensure that a 'First Report of Injury' is submitted to the Labor Commission. If your employer refuses, a workers' comp attorney can file the claim directly for you. Delaying this step can result in a loss of lost wage benefits.

Conclusion

Respiratory injuries are complex because the damage isn't always visible on the surface. By following these steps, you build a foundation that makes it much harder for an insurance company to say your illness isn't work-related. If you are meeting resistance from your employer or their insurance carrier, contact Just Work Comp Law at 801-746-0290 to discuss your situation.
